text: How are the directions of the velocity and force vectors related in a circular motion

text: How are the directions of the velocity and force vectors related in a circular motion

text: How are the directions of the velocity and force vectors related in a circular motion
answer: The equations of motion describe the movement of the center of mass of a body.

text: How are the directions of the velocity and force vectors related in a circular motion
answer: Examples of circular motion include: an artificial satellite orbiting the Earth at constant height, a stone which is tied to a rope and is being swung in circles, a car turning through a curve in a race track , an electron moving perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field , and a gear turning inside a mechanism.

text: How are the directions of the velocity and force vectors related in a circular motion
answer: Since the object's velocity vector is constantly changing direction, the moving object is undergoing acceleration by a centripetal force in the direction of the center of rotation.

text: How are the directions of the velocity and force vectors related in a circular motion
answer: Without this acceleration, the object would move in a straight line, according to Newton's laws of motion .

text: how a vul works
answer: Variable Universal Life Insurance (often shortened to VUL) is a type of life insurance that builds a cash value.

text: how a vul works
answer: In a VUL, the cash value can be invested in a wide variety of separate accounts , similar to mutual funds , and the choice of which of the available separate accounts to use is entirely up to the contract owner.

text: how a vul works
answer: The 'variable' component in the name refers to this ability to invest in separate accounts whose values vary—they vary because they are invested in stock and/or bond markets.

text: how a vul works
answer: The 'universal' component in the name refers to the flexibility the owner has in making premium payments.

text: how a vul works
answer: The premiums can vary from nothing in a given month up to maximums defined by the Internal Revenue Code for life insurance.

text: how a vul works
answer: This flexibility is in contrast to whole life insurance that has fixed premium payments that typically cannot be missed without lapsing the policy (although one may exercise an Automatic Premium Loan feature, or surrender dividends to pay a Whole Life premium).

text: how a vul works
answer: Variable universal life is a type of permanent life insurance , because the death benefit will be paid if the insured dies any time as long as there is sufficient cash value to pay the costs of insurance in the policy.

text: how a vul works
answer: With most if not all VULs, unlike whole life, there is no endowment age (the age at which the cash value equals the death benefit amount, which for whole life is typically 100).

text: how a vul works
answer: This is yet another key advantage of VUL over Whole Life.

text: how a vul works
answer: With a typical whole life policy, the death benefit is limited to the face amount specified in the policy, and at endowment age, the face amount is all that is paid out.

text: how a vul works
answer: Thus with either death or endowment, the insurance company keeps any cash value built up over the years.

text: how a vul works
answer: However, some participating whole life policies offer riders which specify that any dividends paid on the policy be used to purchase "paid up additions" to the policy which increase both the cash value and the death benefit over time.

text: how a vul works
answer: If investments made in the separate accounts out-perform the general account of the insurance company, a higher rate-of-return can occur than the fixed rates-of-return typical for whole life.

text: how a vul works
answer: The combination over the years of no endowment age, continually increasing death benefit, and if a high rate-of-return is earned in the separate accounts of a VUL policy, this could result in higher value to the owner or beneficiary than that of a whole life policy with the same amounts of money paid in as premiums.

text: how many ports are there in networking

text: how many ports are there in networking

text: How will I feel after my Rhizotomy surgery?
answer: This article deals with Selective Dorsal Rhizotomy (SDR) rather than the rhizotomy procedures for pain relief ; for those procedures, which have begun to take the name "rhizotomy" in certain instances, see facet rhizotomy and similar.

text: How will I feel after my Rhizotomy surgery?
answer: A rhizotomy ( ) is a term chiefly referring to a neurosurgical procedure that selectively destroys problematic nerve roots in the spinal cord , most often to relieve the symptoms of neuromuscular conditions such as spastic diplegia and other forms of spastic cerebral palsy .

text: How will I feel after my Rhizotomy surgery?
answer: The selective dorsal rhizotomy (SDR) for spastic cerebral palsy has been the main use of rhizotomy for neurosurgeons specialising in spastic CP since the 1980s; in this surgery, the spasticity-causing nerves are isolated and then targeted and destroyed.

text: How will I feel after my Rhizotomy surgery?
answer: The sensory nerve roots, where spasticity is located, are first separated from the motor ones, and the nerve fibres to be cut are then identified via electromyographic stimulation.

text: How will I feel after my Rhizotomy surgery?
answer: The ones producing spasticity are then selectively lesioned with tiny electrical pulses.

text: How will I feel after my Rhizotomy surgery?
answer: In spasticity, rhizotomy precisely targets and destroys the damaged nerves that don’t receive gamma amino butyric acid , which is the core problem for people with spastic cerebral palsy .

text: How will I feel after my Rhizotomy surgery?
answer: These over-firing, non-GABA-absorbing nerves generate unusual electrical activity during the EMG testing phase in SDR and are thus considered to be the source of the patient's hypertonia ; they are eliminated with the electrical pulses once identified, while the remaining nerves and nerve routes carrying the correct messages remain fully intact and untouched.

text: How will I feel after my Rhizotomy surgery?
answer: This means that the spasticity is permanently dissolved, and that this is done without affecting nervous system sensitivity or function in other areas, because the only nerves destroyed are the over-firing ones responsible for the muscle tightness.

text: How will I feel after my Rhizotomy surgery?
answer: The terms rhizotomy and neurotomy are also increasingly becoming interchangeable in the treatment of chronic back pain from degenerative disc disease .

text: How will I feel after my Rhizotomy surgery?
answer: This is a procedure called a facet rhizotomy and is not a surgical procedure but is instead done on an outpatient basis using a simple probe to apply radiofrequency waves to the impinged pain-causing nerve root lying between the facet joint and the vertebral body.

text: How will I feel after my Rhizotomy surgery?
answer: Such radio frequency nerve lesioning results in five to eight or more months of pain relief before the nerve regenerates and another round of the procedure needs to be performed.

text: How will I feel after my Rhizotomy surgery?
answer: A facet rhizotomy is just one of many different forms of radiofrequency ablation , and its use of the "rhizotomy" name should not be confused with the SDR procedure.

answer: The opinion of the court, written by Chief Justice Roger B. Taney , stirred debate.

text: What did the Supreme Court determine in Dred Scott v. Sandford?

text: What did the Supreme Court determine in Dred Scott v. Sandford?

text: What did the Supreme Court determine in Dred Scott v. Sandford?

text: What did the Supreme Court determine in Dred Scott v. Sandford?
answer: This holding was contrary to the practice of numerous states at the time, particularly Free states , where free blacks did in fact enjoy the rights of citizens, such as the right to vote and hold public office.

text: What did the Supreme Court determine in Dred Scott v. Sandford?

text: What did the Supreme Court determine in Dred Scott v. Sandford?

text: What did the Supreme Court determine in Dred Scott v. Sandford?

text: What did the Supreme Court determine in Dred Scott v. Sandford?

text: What did the Supreme Court determine in Dred Scott v. Sandford?

text: What implication(s) does the law of conservation of matter have for humans?
answer: Antoine Lavoisier 's discovery of the Law of Conservation of Mass led to many new findings in the 19th century.

text: What implication(s) does the law of conservation of matter have for humans?
answer: Joseph Proust 's law of definite proportions and John Dalton 's atomic theory branched from the discoveries of Antoine Lavoisier.

text: What implication(s) does the law of conservation of matter have for humans?
answer: Lavoisier's quantitative experiments revealed that combustion involved oxygen rather than what was previously thought to be phlogiston .

text: What implication(s) does the law of conservation of matter have for humans?
answer: The law of conservation of mass, or principle of mass conservation, states that for any system closed to all transfers of matter and energy (both of which have mass), the mass of the system must remain constant over time, as system mass cannot change quantity if it is not added or removed.

text: What implication(s) does the law of conservation of matter have for humans?
answer: Hence, the quantity of mass is "conserved" over time.

text: What implication(s) does the law of conservation of matter have for humans?
answer: The law implies that mass can neither be created nor destroyed, although it may be rearranged in space, or the entities associated with it may be changed in form, as for example when light or physical work is transformed into particles that contribute the same mass to the system as the light or work had contributed.

text: What implication(s) does the law of conservation of matter have for humans?
answer: The law implies (requires) that during any chemical reaction , nuclear reaction , or radioactive decay in an isolated system, the mass of the reactants or starting material must equal the mass of the products.

text: What implication(s) does the law of conservation of matter have for humans?
answer: The concept of mass conservation is widely used in many fields such as chemistry , mechanics , and fluid dynamics .

text: What implication(s) does the law of conservation of matter have for humans?
answer: Historically, mass conservation was discovered in chemical reactions by Antoine Lavoisier in the late 18th century, and was of crucial importance in the progress from alchemy to the modern natural science of chemistry .

text: What implication(s) does the law of conservation of matter have for humans?
answer: The closely related concept of matter conservation was found to hold good in chemistry to such high approximation that it failed only for the high energies treated by the later refinements of relativity theory, but otherwise remains useful and sufficiently accurate for most chemical calculations, even in modern practice.

text: What implication(s) does the law of conservation of matter have for humans?
answer: In special relativity , needed for accuracy when large energy transfers between systems is involved, the difference between thermodynamically closed and isolated systems becomes important, since conservation of mass is strictly and perfectly upheld only for so-called isolated systems , i.e. those completely isolated from all exchanges with the environment.

text: What implication(s) does the law of conservation of matter have for humans?
answer: In this circumstance, the mass-energy equivalence theorem states that mass conservation is equivalent to total energy conservation, which is the first law of thermodynamics .

text: What implication(s) does the law of conservation of matter have for humans?
answer: By contrast, for a thermodynamically closed system (i.e., one which is closed to exchanges of matter, but open to exchanges of non-material energy, such as heat and work, with the surroundings) mass is (usually) only approximately conserved.

text: What implication(s) does the law of conservation of matter have for humans?
answer: The input or output of non-material energy must change the mass of the system in relativity theory, although the change is usually small, since relatively large amounts of such energy (by comparison with ordinary experience) carry only a small amount of mass (again by ordinary standards of measurement).

text: What implication(s) does the law of conservation of matter have for humans?
answer: In special relativity, mass is not converted to energy, since energy in all of its forms always retains its equivalent amount of mass throughout any transformation or translocation into, or out of, a system.

text: What implication(s) does the law of conservation of matter have for humans?
answer: However, certain types of matter (a different concept) may be converted to energy, so long as the mass of the system is unchanged in the process.

text: What implication(s) does the law of conservation of matter have for humans?
answer: However, if such energy is removed from systems, they must lose the mass that is associated with it.

text: What implication(s) does the law of conservation of matter have for humans?
answer: In general relativity , mass (and energy) conservation in expanding volumes of space is a complex concept, subject to different definitions, and neither mass nor energy is as strictly and simply conserved as is the case in special relativity and in Minkowski space .

text: What implication(s) does the law of conservation of matter have for humans?
answer: For a discussion, see mass in general relativity .

text: what are use taxes?
answer: A use tax in United States is complementary to sales tax which means, if you buy a Taxable product/services without paying any Sales tax to vendor, you owe use tax.

answer: You must self assess use tax and pay it directly to the state where the products were consumed or services were rendered.

text: what are use taxes?
answer: Use tax typically is a tax on consumption but if you buy an item (without paying sales tax) and store it in your warehouse initially, use tax applies to that location where goods were stored.

text: what are use taxes?

text: what are use taxes?
answer: This really means that you don't have to pay both Sales Tax as well as Use Tax on one transaction.

text: what are use taxes?
answer: It is only one or the other that you are required to pay on purchase of taxable products/services.

text: what are use taxes?
answer: If vendor doesn't charge any Sales tax on its invoice then it is the obligation of the buyer to self assess Use tax and pay it to the appropriate Jurisdication.

text: what are use taxes?

text: what are use taxes?
answer: Use tax is also termed as "Consumption Tax" or "Consumer Use Tax".

text: what are use taxes?
answer: A use tax is a type of excise tax levied in the United States by numerous state governments.

text: what are use taxes?
answer: It is assessed upon tangible personal property purchased by a resident of the assessing state for use, storage, or consumption in that state (not for resale), regardless of where the purchase took place.

text: what are use taxes?
answer: If a resident of a state makes a purchase within his home state, full sales tax is paid at the time of the transaction.

text: what are use taxes?
answer: The use tax applies when a resident of the assessing state purchases an item that is not subject to his home state's sales tax.

text: what are use taxes?
answer: Usually, this is due to out-of-state purchases, as well as ordering items through the mail, by phone, or over the Internet from other states.

text: what are use taxes?
answer: The use tax is typically assessed at the same rate as the sales tax that would have been owed (if any) had the same goods been purchased in the state of residence.

text: what are use taxes?
answer: For example, a resident of Massachusetts , with a 6.25% "sales and use tax" on certain goods and services, purchases non-exempt goods or services in New Hampshire for use, storage or other consumption in Massachusetts.

text: what are use taxes?
answer: Under New Hampshire law, the New Hampshire vendor collects no sales taxes on the goods, but the Massachusetts purchaser/user must still pay 6.25% of the sales price directly to the Department of Revenue in Massachusetts as a use tax.

text: what are use taxes?
answer: If the same goods are purchased in a US state that does collect sales tax for such goods at time of purchase, whatever taxes were paid by the purchaser to that state can be deducted (as a tax credit ) from the 6.25% owed for subsequent use, storage or consumption in Massachusetts.

text: what are use taxes?
answer: With few exceptions, no state's vendors will charge the native state's sales tax on goods shipped out of state, meaning all goods ordered from out-of-state are essentially free of sales tax.

text: what are use taxes?
answer: The purchaser is therefore required to declare and pay the use tax to his home state on these ordered goods.

text: what are use taxes?
answer: The assessing jurisdiction may make the use tax payable annually, but some states require a monthly payment.

text: what are use taxes?
answer: For example, where a Vermont resident has not paid at least 6% sales tax on property brought in for use in the state, Vermont law requires filing a tax return (Form SU-452 and payment) by the 20th day of the month following non-exempt purchases to avoid a $50 late fee, a 5% penalty per month, to a maximum of 25%, plus statutory interest on the unpaid tax and penalties.

text: what are use taxes?
answer: Typical exemptions include purchases by charitable non-profit organizations or governmental agencies, purchases for resale in commerce, and purchases via "casual sales" by individuals not in the ordinary course of business.

text: what are use taxes?

text: what are use taxes?

text: what are use taxes?

text: what are use taxes?

text: what are use taxes?
answer: In most cases, this complexity is part of the underlying sales tax laws; but while a brick-and-mortar store has to deal with only the sales tax laws of its own location, remote sellers have to deal with the use tax laws of many jurisdictions—up to every US state and locality that assesses them, if the company has a presence or "nexus" in every state (as large "brick-and-mortar" sellers like Wal-Mart and Best Buy do).

text: what are warehouse spreadsheets used for
answer: In computing , a data warehouse or enterprise data warehouse (DW, DWH, or EDW) is a database used for reporting and data analysis .

answer: It is a central repository of data which is created by integrating data from one or more disparate sources.

text: what are warehouse spreadsheets used for
answer: Data warehouses store current as well as historical data and are used for creating trending reports for senior management reporting such as annual and quarterly comparisons.

answer: The data stored in the warehouse are uploaded from the operational systems (such as marketing, sales etc., shown in the figure to the right).

text: what are warehouse spreadsheets used for
answer: The data may pass through an operational data store for additional operations before they are used in the DW for reporting.

text: what are warehouse spreadsheets used for
answer: The typical ETL -based data warehouse uses staging , data integration, and access layers to house its key functions.

text: what are warehouse spreadsheets used for
answer: The staging layer or staging database stores raw data extracted from each of the disparate source data systems.

text: what are warehouse spreadsheets used for
answer: The integration layer integrates the disparate data sets by transforming the data from the staging layer often storing this transformed data in an operational data store (ODS) database.

text: what are warehouse spreadsheets used for
answer: The integrated data are then moved to yet another database, often called the data warehouse database, where the data is arranged into hierarchical groups often called dimensions and into facts and aggregate facts.

text: what are warehouse spreadsheets used for
answer: The combination of facts and dimensions is sometimes called a star schema .

text: what are warehouse spreadsheets used for
answer: The access layer helps users retrieve data.

text: what are warehouse spreadsheets used for
answer: A data warehouse constructed from an integrated data source systems does not require ETL, staging databases, or operational data store databases.

text: what are warehouse spreadsheets used for
answer: The integrated data source systems may be considered to be a part of a distributed operational data store layer.

text: what are warehouse spreadsheets used for
answer: Data federation methods or data virtualization methods may be used to access the distributed integrated source data systems to consolidate and aggregate data directly into the data warehouse database tables.

text: what are warehouse spreadsheets used for
answer: Unlike the ETL-based data warehouse, the integrated source data systems and the data warehouse are all integrated since there is no transformation of dimensional or reference data.

text: what are warehouse spreadsheets used for
answer: This integrated data warehouse architecture supports the drill down from the aggregate data of the data warehouse to the transactional data of the integrated source data systems.

text: what are warehouse spreadsheets used for
answer: Data warehouses can be subdivided into data marts .

text: what are warehouse spreadsheets used for
answer: Data marts store subsets of data from a warehouse.

text: what are warehouse spreadsheets used for
answer: This definition of the data warehouse focuses on data storage.

text: what are warehouse spreadsheets used for
answer: The main source of the data is cleaned, transformed, cataloged and made available for use by managers and other business professionals for data mining , online analytical processing , market research and decision support (Marakas & O'Brien 2009).

text: what are warehouse spreadsheets used for
answer: However, the means to retrieve and analyze data, to extract, transform and load data, and to manage the data dictionary are also considered essential components of a data warehousing system.

text: what are warehouse spreadsheets used for
answer: Many references to data warehousing use this broader context.

text: what are warehouse spreadsheets used for
answer: Thus, an expanded definition for data warehousing includes business intelligence tools , tools to extract, transform and load data into the repository, and tools to manage and retrieve metadata .

text: what is a synthetic conduit
